skill.name: azure-ai-transcription-py
skill.description: Guidance for building Python speech-to-text workflows with Azure AI Transcription, including batch jobs, real-time streams, timestamps, and diarization.
skill.summary: Build Python transcription workflows with Azure AI speech-to-text examples.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static shell execution findings are false positives from fenced Markdown examples, not runnable skill code. The network and environment-variable findings are legitimate documentation patterns for Azure endpoints and subscription keys, but they are low risk when users manage secrets correctly. No prompt injection, malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or weak cryptographic algorithm evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Credential Handling in Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows Python code reading TRANSCRIPTION_ENDPOINT and TRANSCRIPTION_KEY from environment variables. This is a legitimate configuration pattern, but users must protect subscription keys and avoid logging them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly documents environment-based credential loading for Azure Transcription. The pattern is expected for this SDK and does not include exfiltration or logging.

low_findings.title: External Service URL Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes Azure endpoint and storage URL examples for transcription input. These network references match the stated purpose of using Azure speech services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are direct evidence of network use, but they are placeholder Azure and storage locations. This aligns with the skill description and does not indicate malicious routing.

low_findings.title: Static Execution and Crypto Heuristics Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The detected shell and Python snippets are examples inside Markdown fences. The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at the description line has no supporting code or algorithm evidence.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are documentation examples, not executable skill logic. No file content shows cryptographic code, dynamic execution, prompt injection, or obfuscated behavior.

content.value_statement: Python teams need reliable examples for Azure speech transcription. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through batch and real-time transcription patterns.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the Azure AI Transcription Python package.
Documents required endpoint and subscription key environment variables.
Creates a TranscriptionClient using endpoint and key credentials.
Demonstrates batch transcription with locale, content URLs, and diarization.
Demonstrates real-time audio streaming and event text handling.
Lists practical guidance for timestamps, language selection, and session cleanup.
limitations: Does not include complete application scaffolding or deployment steps.
Does not support DefaultAzureCredential for this client.
Requires users to supply valid Azure resources and storage URLs.
Does not include error handling, retries, or production monitoring examples.

use_cases.title: Meeting Transcript Automation
use_cases.description: Create batch transcription jobs for recorded meetings with speaker diarization and status handling.

use_cases.title: Live Caption Prototype
use_cases.description: Build a real-time transcription loop that streams local audio and reads text events.

use_cases.title: Subtitle Preparation Workflow
use_cases.description: Use timestamps and language selection to prepare transcript output for captions or review.

prompt_templates.title: Install the Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install the Azure AI Transcription Python package and define the required environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Python TranscriptionClient using TRANSCRIPTION_ENDPOINT and TRANSCRIPTION_KEY from environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Build Batch Transcription
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a batch transcription flow for an English meeting recording stored at a secure storage URL.

prompt_templates.title: Design Streaming Transcription
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a real-time transcription loop that sends audio, handles text events, manages backpressure, and closes sessions cleanly.

output_examples.input: User asks for batch transcription guidance for a meeting recording.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains package setup, environment variables, client creation, job submission, diarization, and status checking.

output_examples.input: User asks how to process live audio for captions.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines stream creation, audio file sending, event iteration, text handling, and session cleanup.

output_examples.input: User asks how to improve transcript accuracy.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends specifying locale, enabling diarization for multiple speakers, capturing timestamps, and using suitable audio quality.

best_practices: Store subscription keys in environment variables or a managed secret store.
Use batch transcription for long files stored in cloud storage.
Specify the expected locale and enable diarization when speakers change.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode Azure subscription keys in source files.
Do not send private audio to storage without access controls.
Do not leave streaming sessions open after transcription finishes.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Python workflows for Azure AI batch and real-time speech transcription.

faq.question: Does it include authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows subscription key authentication with endpoint and key environment variables.

faq.question: Can it handle multiple speakers?
faq.answer: Yes. The batch example enables diarization for recordings with multiple speakers.

faq.question: Does it support live transcription?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a real-time stream example that sends audio and reads text events.

faq.question: Does it provide production error handling?
faq.answer: No. Users must add retries, exception handling, logging, and monitoring for production systems.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found low-risk documentation patterns and no malicious behavior.
